pipeline_verified.cargo_bench_no_run_benten_graph: PASS (4 benches compile: get_create_node, durability_modes, concurrent_writers, multi_mb_roundtrip)
pipeline_verified.g3_test_binaries_selected_green: 8/10 green; 2 R3-authored tests fail as flagged in commit message (confirmed by running the subset — see g3-cr-1)

scope: Transaction primitive, change-stream plumbing, system-zone stopgap, ChangeBroadcast, Engine::transaction wiring, WriteContext.pending_ops, and 3 criterion benches (durability_modes, concurrent_writers, multi_mb_roundtrip) + benches/README.md.

findings.claim: G3-A's self-flagged R3 defect is sound — the three failing tests use KVBackend::get/delete with raw CID bytes, which cannot match the G2-established n:CID key schema.
findings.detail: I confirmed failures by running `cargo nextest run -p benten-graph --test transaction_atomicity --test mvcc_snapshot --no-fail-fast`: `tx_commits_all_writes_together`, `tx_two_writes_both_visible_after_commit`, and `delete_then_read_returns_none` all fail on the assertion. The correct shape is `b.get_node(&cid)` / `b.delete_node(&cid)`. There is no documented path where raw CID-bytes are a valid key — the `n:` prefix is crate-private in store.rs (NODE_PREFIX = "n:") and the two `get_by_label` / `put_node_with_context` docstrings route readers through the Node-level API. G3-A correctly avoided editing tests; R4b should fix these with the trivial rename.
findings.suggested_fix: R4b: rename `b.get(cid.as_bytes())` -> `b.get_node(&cid).unwrap().is_some()`, and `b.delete(cid.as_bytes())` -> `b.delete_node(&cid)` in the three cited tests. Purely authoring drift — the API shape G3-A landed is correct.

findings.claim: The `nested_transaction_rejected` test is buggy — it swallows the inner Err via `let nested = tx.begin_nested(); Ok(nested)` rather than `?`-propagating.
findings.detail: Confirmed via `cargo nextest run -p benten-engine --test integration nested_transaction_rejected`: the test panics with 'nested tx must return Err: Err(NestedTransactionNotSupported)' — i.e., the outer engine.transaction returned Ok(Err(NestedTransactionNotSupported)) because the closure returned Ok(nested_result). `begin_nested()` IS wired correctly on EngineTransaction (lib.rs:955-957 returns Err(EngineError::NestedTransactionNotSupported)). The API shape is correct; the test authoring is wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: R4b: change line 34 to `let _nested = tx.begin_nested()?;` and line 35 to `Ok(())` (or `unreachable!()`). Add a second assert-path test that invokes `engine.transaction(...)` recursively from within another `engine.transaction` closure — today only the nested `begin_nested()` call is exercised; the nested top-level `engine.transaction` call shape is not.

findings.claim: Dead imports + discards: `EDGE_SRC_PREFIX` and `EDGE_TGT_PREFIX` are imported at line 38 but never directly used — the file uses only the `edge_src_index_key` / `edge_tgt_index_key` helpers (which internally use the prefixes). Lines 343-345 do `let _ = EDGE_SRC_PREFIX;` etc. to suppress warnings.
findings.detail: The suppression discards are inside `delete_edge` — misleading because a reader assumes the prefixes are load-bearing for the delete logic; they aren't. Remove both the unused imports and the discards together; the code continues to use `edge_src_index_key`/`edge_tgt_index_key` helpers correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `EDGE_SRC_PREFIX, EDGE_TGT_PREFIX` from the import on line 38 and delete lines 343-345. Clippy is currently happy because of the explicit discards, not because the imports are used.

findings.claim: ChangeEvent on delete carries `label: String::new()` — IVM subscribers get no label routing info for delete events.
findings.detail: Phase 1's hand-written IVM views are keyed by CID, so they can clean up by CID lookup without the label. But a future CDC-style consumer or audit-log view that routes by label will receive `label: ""` for every delete and cannot filter. The `PendingOp::DeleteNode`/`DeleteEdge` variants carry only CID, so the info isn't recoverable at fan-out time. This is an acceptable Phase-1 gap but worth documenting on ChangeEvent docstring and adding a proptest that asserts delete events carry CID but empty label.
findings.suggested_fix: R4b/G5 companion: extend PendingOp::DeleteNode to carry `labels: Vec<String>` (read once at delete-time inside the same redb txn — the existing code already reads the Node body to compute index keys at transaction.rs:272-283). Propagate the primary label into the ChangeEvent. Or explicitly document the gap in the ChangeEvent docstring.

findings.claim: Delete paths silently `.ok()` DAG-CBOR decode failures on the read-existing step, swallowing corruption signals.
findings.detail: If a Node body is corrupted (byte flip, incomplete write from an earlier crashed transaction, format drift), `from_slice::<Node>(bytes).ok()` returns None and the delete proceeds to remove the `n:CID` entry WITHOUT removing any index entries. Subsequent reads by label will still match the CID in the label index and surface a 'ghost' Node reference. The idempotent guarantee is preserved but silent corruption masking is a real concern. Minor because in Phase 1 crash recovery is covered by redb's own WAL; graph-layer body corruption is out-of-band. R4b should at least log a warning.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `.ok()` with explicit handling: either log to tracing (once G3/G5 land the tracing dep), or return `GraphError::Decode` and let the caller decide. If kept silent for Phase 1, add a module-level note in transaction.rs acknowledging the edge case.

findings.claim: Transaction primitive bypasses the `begin_write_txn()` helper, so its durability is set inside `Transaction::new` instead of at the txn-open site. Non-DRY but not buggy.
findings.detail: `begin_write_txn()` centralizes durability wiring for the Phase-1 R1 triage `P1.graph.durability` goal — 'every mutating path picks up a durability change automatically'. The transaction primitive calls `self.db.begin_write()` directly (line 750) and then `Transaction::new` sets durability inside (lines 165-167). Current behavior is correct because Transaction::new forwards `self.durability`; but a future change to `begin_write_txn` (e.g., adding tx-id assignment or crash-recovery marker writes) would NOT be picked up by the transaction primitive. Style/DRY concern.
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor `RedbBackend::transaction` and `transaction_with_deny_on_commit` to use `self.begin_write_txn()` and drop the duplicate durability setting in `Transaction::new` (make it take a `redb::WriteTransaction` with durability already set). Or accept the duplication and add a comment at the `begin_write` callsite explaining why it skips the helper.

findings.claim: Transaction primitive has no privileged-path escape hatch — engine-internal code (grant_capability / create_view / revoke_capability, G7) that needs to write system-zone nodes through a transaction cannot do so today.
findings.detail: `Transaction::new` accepts `is_privileged: bool`, but `RedbBackend::transaction` and `transaction_with_deny_on_commit` both hard-code `false`. G7 will need to land a `transaction_privileged` (or `transaction_with_context(ctx)`) entry point so engine-internal multi-write operations (e.g., a capability grant that writes a CapabilityGrant Node + a GRANTED_TO edge atomically) can opt into the privileged path. G3-A's scope boundary is defensible (G3 is user-level transactions), but the internal asymmetry is worth explicitly noting in a doc comment now so G7 doesn't rediscover the gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level doc note in transaction.rs: 'Phase 1 transactions are always non-privileged; an engine-internal privileged-transaction entry point will land with G7 for grant/create_view/revoke atomic paths.' Plus a TODO(G7) marker on the hard-coded `false`.

findings.claim: ChangeEvent.label carries only labels[0]. G2 fix-pass deferred the rename to labels: Vec<String>; G3-A kept the deferral. For Phase 1 hand-written IVM this is acceptable, but the compromise should be re-evaluated BEFORE G5 subscribes.
findings.detail: The store.rs docstring at lines 236-242 is honest about the asymmetry (on-disk label index emits one entry per label; ChangeEvent carries only primary). CLAUDE.md confirms Phase-1 hand-written IVM views don't use multi-label nodes. However, the 5 baseline IVM views include governance-inheritance and capability-grant-resolution which MIGHT cross the multi-label boundary in practice. G5 dispatch should verify this explicitly before extending ChangeEvent. If G5 confirms none of the 5 views touch multi-label nodes, the deferral stands; if any view does, land the rename NOW rather than paper over the asymmetry in view-build code. Not blocking G3 commit.
findings.suggested_fix: G5 dispatch prompt should include: 'Before building views, verify ChangeEvent.label = labels[0] is sufficient for each of the 5 baseline views. If not, rename to labels: Vec<String> and update the ~10 R3 test sites in one pass.' Alternatively, add a regression proptest now that asserts multi-label nodes emit ChangeEvent.label == labels[0] (pins the compromise explicitly).

findings.claim: Method name is mildly misleading: there is no config — it ALWAYS denies. A future caller expecting to pass in a deny predicate could miscall it.
findings.detail: The docstring (lines 776-794) is explicit about the test-hook nature, but the public-on-RedbBackend surface means a caller who reads only the signature might expect a configurable deny behavior. Recommend either (a) renaming to `transaction_always_denies_at_commit_for_test` to make intent crystal clear, (b) moving to a `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "testing"))]` gate so it's not part of the production public surface, or (c) minimally adding the phrase 'ALWAYS denies' to the first sentence of the docstring so it's visible in rustdoc summaries.
findings.suggested_fix: At minimum, change the doc's first sentence from 'Transaction variant used by the commit-denial edge-case test' to 'Transaction variant that ALWAYS denies at commit time, used by...'. Better: gate behind `#[cfg(feature = "testing")]` with the feature enabled in dev-dependencies only.

findings.claim: Closure-error and capability-denial branches both produce `GraphError::TxAborted { reason: ... }` from the inner backend.transaction call, which the outer then discards in favor of user_result. The `reason` strings (`"capability denied"`, `"closure error"`) are throw-away. Non-blocking but cleanable.
findings.detail: The two-pass unwrap (backend returns TxAborted, engine discards and prefers user_result) works correctly but is the sort of control flow that invites bugs if someone adds a third branch (e.g., IVM-rebuild error) without also saving into user_result. Consider an explicit enum over the closure's exit state (Ok(R) / PolicyDenied(CapError) / ClosureErr(EngineError)) passed back to the outer layer, so the invariant 'user_result is always Some when tx_outcome is Err(TxAborted)' is encoded in types rather than in a prose comment on line 556-558. Not blocking.
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor to a single exit enum; or at minimum add a debug_assert in the `unreachable!` arm to catch regressions in dev builds.

findings.claim: `GraphTxLike` shim is an object-safe workaround for the lifetime of `benten_graph::Transaction<'a>`. Currently exposes only `put_node`; every other EngineTransaction API (put_edge, delete_node, delete_edge, update_node) is absent — these are the G7 `todo!()` gaps G3-A flagged in the commit message.
findings.detail: Acceptable division of labor for G3 (the point of G3 is transactions-for-creates; G7 owns the full CRUD surface). But the public `EngineTransaction::put_node` on the Engine-facing API documents only put. A test in `system_zone_api_exclusivity::transaction_cannot_smuggle_system_zone_write` happens to exercise only put. G7 should extend GraphTxLike with put_edge/delete_* and the full EngineTransaction surface at the same commit.
findings.suggested_fix: No action now — but the G7 dispatch prompt should name the GraphTxLike surface expansion explicitly (put_edge, delete_node, delete_edge), and flag that the current `ops_collector.push(PutNode{...})` pattern needs a parallel `PutEdge`/`DeleteNode`/`DeleteEdge` in the engine layer (PendingOp already has the variants).

findings.claim: fan_out holds no lock on the subscribers list while invoking callbacks (the list is cloned before the loop), which is the right choice — but the clone happens on EVERY commit, even when zero subscribers are registered. Micro-optimization.
findings.detail: Lines 758-761: `let subs = { let guard = self.subscribers.lock(); guard.clone() };` — unconditional clone of a Vec<Arc<dyn ChangeSubscriber>>. For the thinness-test case (no IVM subscribers), this is a Vec::clone of an empty vec (cheap, but still a lock acquisition per commit). Phase 1 commit rate is 200-300 writes/sec Immediate, so even 300 needless mutex acquisitions/sec is trivial — not worth optimizing. But if a bench shows a contention ceiling, this is the first place to look.
findings.suggested_fix: Guard the clone with an `is_empty` check: `let subs = { let g = self.subscribers.lock()...; if g.is_empty() { continue/skip } else { g.clone() } }`. Better: wrap the subscribers in `ArcSwap<Vec<...>>` in Phase 2 so the fan-out path is entirely lock-free.

findings.claim: publish clones the subscribers Vec under a short lock, then loops invoking. Then separately catch_unwinds each invocation. Each catch_unwind allocates a closure frame; event is `event.clone()` per subscriber (unnecessary on the outer side if the callback already takes &ChangeEvent).
findings.detail: Line 111: `let event = event.clone();` — wrapped inside the AssertUnwindSafe closure to make it 'Send-friendly' across the catch boundary. But `catch_unwind` does not cross threads; it catches a panic on the same thread. The clone is only needed if the closure takes event by value; the trait shape is `Fn(&ChangeEvent)`, which takes by reference. Dropping the clone would halve allocation cost per subscribed callback. AssertUnwindSafe doesn't require move ownership; `catch_unwind` runs the closure on the current thread.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `|| { cb_clone(&event); }` with a version that borrows `event` directly: catch_unwind(AssertUnwindSafe(|| cb_clone(event))) — just pass the `&ChangeEvent` reference through. Measure with the concurrent-writers bench if the change is load-bearing.

findings.claim: GraphError::code wildcards Redb/Decode to `ErrorCode::Unknown("graph_internal")` — loses actionable error codes in the public catalog surface.
findings.detail: The existing doc at GraphError::Redb (line 54-56) already tracks the `#[from] redb::Error` refactor as a Phase-1 follow-up (R4b). The `graph_internal` fallback is a reasonable stopgap. But user code matching on `engine_error.code() == "E_UNKNOWN"` will lose the ability to distinguish genuine unknown errors from redb failures. Consider a dedicated `ErrorCode::RedbIO` variant or at least mapping to one of the existing catalog codes (e.g., `E_SERIALIZE` for Decode, something new for Redb). The R4b note covers the refactor; just ensure the code-surface mapping lands at the same time.
findings.suggested_fix: When the R4b `#[from] redb::Error` refactor lands, also add dedicated ErrorCode variants (e.g., `RedbBackendIo`, `OnDiskCorruption`) and map them 1:1. Update the ERROR-CATALOG.md accordingly.

findings.claim: register_subscriber has no deregister pair — subscribers stick around for the backend's lifetime. For long-lived engines this is fine; for tests that spin up and tear down subscribers repeatedly, this is a small leak.
findings.detail: R3 test `system_zone_api_exclusivity::transaction_cannot_smuggle_system_zone_write` and the broadcast tests use a fresh tempdir backend per test, so dropping the backend releases all subscribers. But a long-lived Engine with N transient subscribers (e.g., a test harness that drains after each call) would grow unboundedly. Phase 1 scope doesn't require deregistration, but the Arc-based shape makes it natural: return a `Subscription` handle that, on drop, removes itself from the subscribers list. G5 may want this.
findings.suggested_fix: Document the 'subscribers live for the backend's lifetime' contract in the register_subscriber docstring. Track 'add deregister(handle)' as a G5 follow-up if IVM needs transient subscribers.

positive_observations: TxGuard RAII (transaction.rs:113-139) is clean and correct — the lock-release-on-drop behavior is exactly what's needed for panic-safe nested-tx rejection.
Panic containment in fan_out (transaction.rs:374-387) correctly uses AssertUnwindSafe around per-subscriber calls so one misbehaving subscriber cannot poison the commit thread.

system-zone guard is correctly duplicated in both the non-transactional put_node_with_context (redb_backend.rs:702-717) AND the in-transaction Transaction::put_node (transaction.rs:187-196) — no way to smuggle a system-labeled write through the transaction primitive.

The engine-layer Engine::transaction correctly fires CapabilityPolicy::check_write EXACTLY ONCE per transaction (not per write), and ONLY after the closure returns Ok — matching the R1 named-compromise #1 TOCTOU contract.
Empty transactions skip the capability check entirely (lib.rs:518) — correct behavior per the documented contract.

PendingOp enum is well-modeled — the four-variant (PutNode/PutEdge/DeleteNode/DeleteEdge) shape gives policies enough info to route denials without forcing benten-caps to take a dep on benten-graph (two parallel PendingOps, translated at the engine-layer bridge).
ChangeBroadcast correctly has NO tokio dep (verified by `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -p benten-graph` succeeding) — the plan's R1 architect ratification is preserved.
benches/README.md (185 lines) is honest about the Group-collapse-to-Immediate Phase-1 compromise and the 4.1ms vs <500µs macOS APFS miss; interpretation table is genuinely useful.
All benches compile clean with --measurement-time 2 (verified via `cargo bench --no-run`).
SnapshotHandle::get_node is a thin, correct wrapper over a held redb::ReadTransaction; Drop impl releases cleanly. scan_label stays correctly a G6 stub.
No tests were edited by G3-A (verified via `git show 90f0f2f --stat | grep tests/` — empty output). TDD discipline preserved.
New GraphError variants (SystemZoneWrite, NestedTransactionNotSupported, TxAborted) correctly route through GraphError::code() to the matching ErrorCode variants.
Closure panic path (transaction.rs:134-139 TxGuard Drop) correctly releases the in-tx flag even on unwinding — confirmed by the `tx_closure_panic_rolls_back_and_repropagates` test passing.
notes: G3 delivers the transaction primitive, change-notification stream, system-zone stopgap, and the three benches in scope. The implementation is correct on all the generic-audit concerns I flagged (panic-safety, check-write-at-commit, subscriber-after-commit-only, no-tokio-dep, system-zone-guard in both txn and non-txn paths). The 15 findings are ALL minor — mostly test-authoring drift that G3-A correctly declined to fix per TDD policy (g3-cr-1, g3-cr-2), cleanup items (g3-cr-3 dead imports, g3-cr-6 DRY), and forward-looking concerns for G5/G7/R4b that should be docket items rather than blockers (g3-cr-4, g3-cr-7, g3-cr-8, g3-cr-11). Pipeline is clean: fmt+clippy+check+wasm+bench-no-run all green. The 3 R3 test failures flagged in the commit message are genuine R3 authoring drift, not G3-A API-shape errors — confirmed by running the failing tests and reading their assertion paths. Recommend PASS for G3 with g3-cr-1 and g3-cr-2 added to the R4b test-fix list.
